seo.pageTitle: Jeju Winter Tour: Tangerine, Snow & Camellia | AtoC Korea
seo.metaDescription: Winter-only 9-hour Jeju small-group tour: Hallasan snow viewpoint, Camellia Hill peak bloom, hands-on tangerine picking, Jusangjeolli, Cheonjeyeon Falls. Dec–Feb.
seo.title: Jeju Winter Tour: Tangerine, Snow & Camellia | AtoC Korea
seo.description: Jeju winter southwest small-group tour: Hallasan snow hike → tangerine farm picking (Hallabong peak Dec–Feb) → Camellia Hill camellia season → Jusangjeolli cliff → Cheonjeyeon Waterfall. Dec–Feb only.

catalog_card.title: Jeju Winter: Tangerine, Snow & Camellia
catalog_card.shortCardDescription: A seasonal winter day-tour of southwest Jeju (December through February) built around the three things only Jeju does well in winter: snow on Hallasan's slopes, red camellias in full bloom at Camellia Hill, and pick-your-own Jeju tangerines (gyul) at a working orchard. The route also covers Cheonjeyeon Falls, Jusangjeolli columnar cliffs, and the Osulloc Tea Museum. Hotel pickup in Jeju City, English/Chinese-speaking licensed guide.
catalog_card.subtitle: Snow on Hallasan, peak-bloom Camellia Hill, hands-on tangerine picking, plus Jusangjeolli and Cheonjeyeon — Jeju's winter southwest in 9 hours.

hero.tagline: The three things only Jeju does well in winter — snow, camellia, and citrus

itineraryStops.name: Hotel Pickup (Jeju City / Jungmun area)
itineraryStops.description:
**Hotel pickup 08:30-09:00** from designated Jeju City or Jungmun-area hotels. The vehicle arrives at the hotel lobby with your guide and driver. Standard pickup includes central Jeju City, Jeju Airport-area accommodations, and the Jungmun Tourist Complex. 

Guests outside the standard area may join from a meeting point or request paid pickup if available. Mountain conditions can change quickly in winter, so the guide checks Hallasan road and trail conditions before departure.
highlights: **Pickup 08:30-09:00** from Jeju City and Jungmun hotels
Minicoach (8–15 pax shared); English-speaking guide + driver
Three off-hotel meeting points: Bus Terminal, Halla Dept Store, Yeon-dong Crossroads
**Winter departure can shift +15 min** if overnight snowfall on Route 1100 reported
Crampons rentable at Hallasan trailhead (~₩8,000); operator carries spare sets
Hot tea/coffee available in vehicle throughout the day
itineraryStops.whyOnRoute: Pickup is timed so the group can reach Hallasan before winter trail cutoffs and changing mountain conditions. Covering both Jeju City and Jungmun helps guests join from the island's two main accommodation areas while still keeping the morning route focused.

sources: AtoC Korea operations notes (Jeju winter dispatch protocol)

itineraryStops.name: Hallasan Eorimok Trail (Winter Snow Hike)
itineraryStops.description:
**Eorimok Trail** (어리목 탐방로) is one of **two easiest of Hallasan National Park's 5 trails** (the others requiring advance reservation include Seongpanak and Gwaneumsa for summit access — Eorimok and Yeongsil are the **non-summit, no-reservation routes** that are always available). 

The trail leads **4.7 km one-way to Witseoreum Shelter (1,700 m elevation)**, the official turnaround at the Witseoreum plateau — **NOT the summit** (1,950 m Baengnokdam crater is reservation-only via Seongpanak/Gwaneumsa). 

For a **9-hour day-tour**, the standard winter-season practice is to hike **1.5–2 km in** (~45 min one-way) to reach the **forest-to-plateau transition** where dwarf bamboo fields open up to dramatic Hallasan-summit-wall views, then return to the trailhead — covering ~3–4 km total in ~90 min hiking time + ~30 min trail buffer. 

**Winter conditions** (November–March): the trail's most dramatic season — **snow-encrusted dwarf trees create a 'frozen guardian' landscape** widely photographed by Korean hiking clubs who plan last-minute trips after fresh snowfalls; **hoarfrost** + occasional **sea-of-clouds below the ridge** + mountain-side snow drifts make this Jeju's signature winter photo. 

**Crampons recommended November–March** (basic strap-on sets sold at the trailhead ~₩8,000 / ~$8 USD; the local team also carries 2–3 spare sets in-vehicle). 

**FREE admission** (Hallasan National Park has no entry fee); **opens 05:00, last entry ~11:30 in winter** (vs ~12:30 other seasons — the early winter cutoff is the day's hard schedule schedule limit). 

**25 min drive from Jeju City** via Route 1100; free trailhead car park (fills on weekends — the local team schedules early arrival in peak season). Bus access also available via **Bus 240** from Jeju Bus Terminal.
highlights: **Eorimok = 1 of 2 easiest Hallasan trails** + **no reservation required** (unlike summit-access Seongpanak/Gwaneumsa)
**4.7 km to Witseoreum Shelter (1,700 m)** — non-summit; tour groups typically go 1.5–2 km in
**FREE admission** — Hallasan National Park has no entry fee
**Last entry ~11:30 in winter** (vs ~12:30 other seasons) — hard day-schedule schedule limit
**'Frozen guardians'** — snow-encrusted dwarf trees in winter; signature Jeju winter photo
**Hoarfrost + sea-of-clouds + summit-wall views** on clear winter days
**Crampons required Nov–Mar** — sold at trailhead ~₩8,000; operator carries spares
**25 min drive from Jeju City** via Route 1100 (Korea's highest national highway)
Bus 240 access from Jeju Bus Terminal; Bus 1100/1100-1 weekend service
itineraryStops.whyOnRoute: **Hallasan Eorimok is the day's altitude and winter-snow highlight.** Jeju's winter scenery is most visible on the upper slopes, and Eorimok gives guests a realistic taste of Hallasan without the summit-reservation commitment. The exact walking depth is adjusted to weather, trail surface, and group comfort.

sources: Hallasan National Park / english.jeju.go.kr (trail length, elevation, hours)
AllTrails (Eorimok trail mileage variants 8.7 mi / 7 mi point-to-point with Yeongsil)
Hunting for Bliss / Fork and Foot (winter trail conditions, 'frozen guardian' landscape, hike timing)

smartNotes.photo: Snow-covered dwarf birch trees along the upper ridge are the iconic winter shot; arrive before 10:00 for undisturbed snowfields
smartNotes.facilities: Visitor center with snacks and information
smartNotes.tip: Bring layers — temperature is 5–10°C cooler than coastal Jeju; check road conditions in winter; combine with Cheonjeyeon Falls if descending south

visitBasics.hours: Entry allowed from sunrise to 13:00 (last entry cutoff in winter); no summit access in winter
visitBasics.closed: 1100 Highway may close temporarily during heavy snow (Dec–Feb)
visitBasics.walking: Moderate–Strenuous — 4.7 km one-way to Witseoreum Shelter (1,700 m elevation); return same route; allow 5–6 hours round-trip

itineraryStops.name: Tangerine (Gyul) Picking Experience — Winter Hallabong Peak
itineraryStops.description:
**Jeju tangerine picking experience** at a carefully selected farm in the Seogwipo, Namwon-eup, or Jungmun area. The farm is chosen based on harvest condition, travel date, and availability. Winter is Jeju's key citrus season, and guests can usually pick, taste, and take home a small amount of fruit according to the farm's rules. 

Greenhouse or sheltered areas may be used on rainy days, depending on the selected farm.
highlights: **Jeju citrus tradition over 1,500 years old** — royal gift since Baekje period (4th–7th c)
**Hallabong peak mid-Dec to mid-Jan** — most famous variety, easy-peel + juicy + sweet
**Eat-as-you-pick unlimited on-farm**; take-home weighed at ₩2,500–15,000/kg
**Entry ₩5,000–15,000/pax** (often includes 1 kg take-home)
**7 featured varieties**: Hallabong / Cheonhyehyang / Hwanggeumhyang / Red Hyang / Gamgyul / Hagyul / Dangyuja
**Gyulhyangi Farm** — English-friendly staff + complimentary tangerine tea/jam tasting
**Jeju Southernmost Theme Park** — GAP-certified, year-round greenhouse, monorail + petting zoo
**Family-friendly** — flat orchard paths, stroller-accessible, kid-favorite
**Rain backup** — most farms have greenhouse picking sections
itineraryStops.whyOnRoute: **Tangerine picking is the day's hands-on winter experience.** It fits naturally after the Hallasan stop, when guests are ready for a warmer and easier activity before lunch. Farm selection depends on the date and fruit condition, so the goal is to provide a stable picking experience across the winter season rather than relying on one fixed orchard.

sources: VisitKorea / english.visitkorea.or.kr (Jeju citrus history; royal-gift tradition since Baekje)
Jeju Provincial Government (Hallabong + Cheonhyehyang + Hwanggeumhyang + Red Hyang varieties)
third-party booking site / / (farm rotation: Gyulhyangi, Bong Bong, Choenamdan, Jeju Southernmost, Jejue In; pricing ₩5,000–15,000)
Korea Agricultural GAP Certification (Jeju Southernmost Theme Park year-round greenhouse)

itineraryStops.name: Lunch (Southern Jeju local restaurant)
itineraryStops.description:
**Lunch at a guide-vetted southern Jeju restaurant** — typically in the Seogwipo or Andeok-myeon area, fitting the day's geographic flow (between tangerine farm and Camellia Hill). 

**Two typical options** depending on group preference and seasonal availability: **(1) Black Pork BBQ** (제주 흑돼지) — Jeju's signature ingredient; the indigenous **Jeju Black Pig was designated Natural Monument of Korea in 2015**, and pork-belly cuts are grilled over charcoal at the table with traditional banchan (winter banchan often includes Jeju winter cabbage kimchi + radish + Mom-guk seaweed soup); or **(2) Galchi-jorim** (갈치조림 — braised hairtail with radish, soy-chili sauce) — winter is the **Jeju hairtail peak season** (Nov–Feb), with the season's deepest fish from the southern coast reaching tables within hours of dockside landing. 

**Vegetarian options** available at most venues with advance notice (Bibimbap with Jeju seasonal vegetables + Mom-guk; the guide confirms ~30 min ahead). 

**Winter signature beverages**: hot **yujacha** (citron tea — same Jeju citrus family as Hallabong), **omegisul** (Seongeup family-recipe rice-millet liquor — winter warming spirit), and the local **Hallasan Soju** (~₩5,000). All venues in the rotation accept **major cards (Visa/Mastercard) + cash KRW**; bilingual photo menus (English/Chinese) are standard. 

**Lunch is NOT included** in standard pricing — typical out-of-pocket **₩15,000–25,000/pax**; inclusive lunch upgrade available on booking. **Winter rain backup**: all rotation venues have indoor seating, so a southern-Jeju winter shower never disrupts the meal.
highlights: Guide-vetted Seogwipo / Andeok-myeon-area restaurant
**Black Pork BBQ** (Natural Monument 2015) or **winter Galchi-jorim** (Nov–Feb hairtail peak)
Vegetarian options (Bibimbap + Mom-guk) on advance notice
**Yujacha** (citron tea) + **Omegisul** (winter warming spirit) signature beverages
Card + cash accepted; bilingual photo menus
**Lunch NOT included** — ₩15,000–25,000/pax out-of-pocket
All venues have indoor seating — winter rain backup
itineraryStops.whyOnRoute: **Lunch positioning at 13:15 absorbs any morning Hallasan + tangerine schedule slip** — by this point in winter, the Eorimok trail and orchard schedules are behind the group, and lunch acts as flex buffer before the afternoon's three substantive stops. Geographic positioning between tangerine farm (Namwon-eup area) and Camellia Hill (Andeok-myeon) means **zero detour cost** — the lunch venue rotation is on the natural drive line.

sources: Jeju Black Pig Natural Monument designation (2015, Cultural Heritage Administration)
Visit Jeju (winter seafood — galchi-jorim Nov–Feb peak)
AtoC Korea operations notes (winter southern Jeju lunch venue rotation)

itineraryStops.name: Camellia Hill (year-round signature flower garden)
itineraryStops.category: Botanical Garden — Largest Camellia Arboretum in the East
itineraryStops.description:
**Camellia Hill** is a large botanical garden in Andeok known for camellias, seasonal flowers, photo paths, and greenhouse-style garden spaces. VisitJeju describes the park as holding about 6,000 camellia trees and over 500 species of wildflowers, trees, and other plants. 

In winter, camellias are the main highlight, and the garden offers a colorful contrast to Hallasan's colder mountain scenery.
highlights: **172,000 m²** — largest camellia arboretum in the East
**6,000 trees / 500+ species from 80 countries** — accumulated 30+ years
**Year-round bloom calendar** — Nov–Mar camellias, Mar–May cherry + tulip, Jun–Aug hydrangea
**Themed sections**: European Garden, Korean Native Forest, Cherry Walk, Hydrangea Field
Glass **greenhouse + gallery + cafe** complex — indoor refuge (15% of visit)
**'EXchange / Transit Love 3'** filming location — Ju-won & Yu-jeong's date scene
**Open 365 days**; closes at sundown (no outdoor lighting)
Admission **₩12,000 adult / ₩10,000 youth+senior / ₩9,000 child (4–11) / FREE under 3**
**~85% outdoor** garden walking — comfortable shoes recommended
itineraryStops.whyOnRoute: **Camellia Hill is the day's winter-flower highlight.** The garden works especially well in the colder months because camellias bring strong color when many outdoor landscapes feel quieter. Its location near the south and southwest route also keeps the afternoon drive manageable after lunch and the tangerine farm.

sources: Korea Tourism Organization — Top Korean botanical gardens registry
Kakao TV / Wavve programming archive — 'EXchange / Transit Love 3' filming locations

smartNotes.photo: Hydrangea tunnel in June; pink muhly grass field in October; camellia varieties peak Nov–Apr
smartNotes.facilities: Indoor greenhouse café, gift shop, picnic areas
smartNotes.tip: Visit before 10am or after 4pm in peak season to avoid crowds; pair with Osulloc (10 min drive)

convenience.restroom: Available at entrance and within garden

visitBasics.admission: Adult ₩12,000 / Teen & Senior ₩10,000 / Child (4–11) ₩9,000 / Under 3 free
visitBasics.walking: Easy to moderate — gentle hill paths; mostly stroller-accessible

itineraryStops.name: Daepo Jusangjeolli Cliff
itineraryStops.description:
**Daepo Jusangjeolli Cliff** is a dramatic stretch of columnar basalt on the Jungmun coast. The stone pillars were formed as lava cooled and contracted, creating the polygonal columns seen along the sea. 

The columns are commonly described as 30-40 m high and continue for about 1 km along the coast. Visitors use a clifftop boardwalk and viewing decks to see the columns, ocean, and wave action. 

On windy or high-tide days, waves may create strong spray, so the guide may adjust photo time for safety and comfort.
highlights: **Natural Monument No. 443** — designated **2005-01-06**, the most recent Jeju coastal NM
Columns rise **30–40 m**, packed in **5- to 7-sided polygons** (mostly hexagons) along **1 km** of coastline
Formation: **Hallasan flank lava cooled in the sea ~250,000–140,000 years ago**, contracting perpendicular to cooling face
**East Asia's premier columnar-jointing site** — peer to Giant's Causeway, Devils Postpile, Fingal's Cave
**Strong wave spray on some high-tide days** — dramatic coastal photos when conditions are safe
**0.8 km boardwalk loop**, ~15 min walk — barrier-free deck, wheelchair/stroller accessible
Part of **Jeju Global Geopark (UNESCO 2010-10)** — 13 island-wide geosites
Admission **₩2,000 adult / ₩1,000 youth / FREE under 7 + over 65**
Hallasan summit visible to the north on clear days, **directly downstream** geologically
timeUsed: Walk to first viewing platform — ~5 min
itineraryStops.whyOnRoute: **Jusangjeolli Cliff is the day's coastal geology highlight.** After Hallasan and the garden stops, the cliff shows a very different side of Jeju's volcanic landscape. Winter waves can make the coast feel more dramatic, but viewing time is adjusted to wind and sea conditions for safety.

sources: Cultural Heritage Administration — Natural Monument No. 443 designation (2005-01-06)
UNESCO Global Geoparks Network — Jeju (2010 designation)
Korea Geological Survey — Jeju basalt formation studies

smartNotes.photo: Best with high tide and rough seas — waves smashing into the columns make dramatic shots; golden hour gives best column-color contrast
smartNotes.facilities: Small geological exhibition near entrance; souvenir shop
smartNotes.tip: Get there before 11am or after 4pm to avoid tour-bus crowds; combine with Jungmun Saekdal Beach (5 min) for a half-day

visitBasics.walking: Easy — flat wooden boardwalk along cliff; total walk 15–20 min

itineraryStops.name: Cheonjeyeon Waterfall
itineraryStops.category: Three-Tier Waterfall + Forest — 'Pond of the Emperor of Heaven'
itineraryStops.description:
**Cheonjeyeon Falls (천제연, '연 = pond + 천제 = Emperor of Heaven')** is a **three-tier waterfall** in Jungmun's volcanic gorge, designated **Natural Monument No. 378 (1993)** for its rare ecological combination. 

The name comes from a **Joseon-era Daoist legend that seven nymphs (선녀, *seonnyeo*) descended from heaven nightly to bathe in the upper pond** — a myth reinforced by the dramatic site's microclimate of perpetual mist. 

The **1st tier (22 m fall, 21 m deep pond)** drops into a circular basalt cauldron and only flows after heavy rain — most days the upper pond sits glassy and reflective. 

The **2nd tier (30 m fall) flows year-round** — the dominant photographic subject. The 3rd tier completes the descent to the sea. The **120 m / 78 m-high Seonimgyo Bridge (선임교, 'Bridge of the Seven Nymphs')** crosses the gorge midway through the loop — its cast-bronze relief panels along both sides depict **all seven nymphs in flight**, a literal architectural realization of the founding legend. 

Surrounding forest is a **rare-plant habitat** including **Korean toadlily (***Tricyrtis macropoda***)** and the endangered **Mankyua chejuense fern** (Jeju endemic, IUCN Critically Endangered). Loop length: **0.8 km, ~30 min walk** with several steep stair sections. 

Admission: **Adult ₩2,500 / Youth ₩1,350 / FREE under 7 + over 65**. Cheonjeyeon is also a member of the **'Yeongjusipgyeong (영주십경, 10 Scenic Wonders of Jeju)'** Joseon-era literati canon — alongside Hallasan summit, Seongsan Sunrise, and Jeongbang Falls — meaning today's southwest loop covers **2 of the 10 (Hallasan + Cheonjeyeon)** in a single day. 

The biennial **Jeju Chilseonyeo (Seven Nymphs) Festival** held at the bridge in May (every odd year) reenacts the descent legend with traditional dance and musical performance — reservations required when the festival coincides with a tour date. Hours **09:00–18:00** year-round (last entry 17:30).
highlights: **Natural Monument No. 378 (designated 1993)** — three-tier waterfall + rare-plant habitat
Name **'Cheonjeyeon' = 'Pond of the Emperor of Heaven'** — Joseon Daoist legend
**Seven Nymphs (선녀) legend** carved into the **120 m × 78 m Seonimgyo Bridge** relief panels
**1st tier (22 m / 21 m pond)** — only flows after heavy rain; **2nd tier (30 m) flows year-round**
Habitat for **Mankyua chejuense fern** — Jeju endemic, IUCN Critically Endangered
**0.8 km loop / ~30 min** — moderate stairs, footwear matters
Admission **₩2,500 adult / ₩1,350 youth / FREE under 7 + over 65**
**Jeju Chilseonyeo (Seven Nymphs) Festival** held biennially at the site — bridge ceremony
**Misty microclimate** — natural cooling, popular summer afternoon retreat
timeUsed: Descent to 1st tier viewing platform — ~10 min
Seonimgyo Bridge + nymph relief panels — ~15 min
Forest loop + ascent back to coach — ~25 min
itineraryStops.whyOnRoute: **Cheonjeyeon Falls is the final scenery stop.** It is a three-tier waterfall area in Jungmun, known for its forested gorge and Seonimgyo Bridge. The stop is close to Jusangjeolli, so it completes the south-coast section without adding a long drive before evening drop-off.

sources: Cultural Heritage Administration — Natural Monument No. 378 (1993 designation)
Korea National Arboretum — Mankyua chejuense conservation records
Jeju Cultural Foundation — Seven Nymphs Festival history
Seogwipo City tourism — Cheonjeyeon admission and access

smartNotes.photo: The second tier from its viewing deck is the iconic 30m waterfall shot; Seonimgyo Bridge with seven nymphs is also striking
smartNotes.facilities: Small souvenir shop and café at entrance
smartNotes.tip: The first tier is dry except after heavy rain — don't be surprised if it's not flowing; allow 1.5–2 hours to see all three tiers; do NOT confuse with Cheonjiyeon Falls (different falls in Seogwipo)

convenience.restroom: At entrance/parking area only — none past the ticket office

visitBasics.walking: Moderate — many stairs through the valley; not stroller-friendly; mobility-difficult

itineraryStops.name: Drop-off (Jeju City / Jungmun area)
itineraryStops.description:
**Return drive to original pickup hotels** in Jeju City and Jungmun-area accommodations. The route from Cheonjeyeon Falls (Jungmun) splits — **Jungmun-area hotels are dropped off first** (~5 min from the falls), then the minicoach continues to Jeju City via Highway 1135 (~50 min). 

Drop-off is **typically at the original pickup hotel lobby**; guests who joined at off-hotel meeting points are returned to the same point unless otherwise requested. 

**Winter dispatch consideration**: if Route 1100 (the Hallasan ascent road used in the morning) has accumulated snow or freezing rain by evening, the return uses **alternative Route 1135 + 16** to avoid altitude exposure on the ride home. 

**Vehicle-side amenities**: hot tea/coffee remains available in the in-vehicle cooler; the guide can answer follow-up questions about Jeju (geology, citrus history, Korean dating-show filming, food tradition) during the return drive — a small-group day-tour's natural conversational close. 

If guests have **next-day Jeju International Airport flights**, the guide can advise on **Bus 600** from Jungmun-area hotels (~75 min to airport) or downtown Jeju City taxi/Bus 100 connections (~30 min). Tip-jar protocol: guides do not solicit gratuity, but a customary tip range of **₩10,000–20,000/pax** is appreciated for full-day winter tours (cash or KakaoPay).
highlights: **Jungmun hotels dropped first** (~5 min from Cheonjeyeon), then Jeju City (~50 min)
Return at original pickup hotel lobby or off-hotel meeting point
**Winter alternative routing** (Route 1135 + 16) when Route 1100 has overnight ice
Guide-led conversational close — Jeju Q&A during return drive
**Bus 600** advised for next-day airport connections from Jungmun
Customary tip range ₩10,000–20,000/pax (not solicited; cash or KakaoPay)
itineraryStops.whyOnRoute: The drop-off plan keeps Jungmun hotels and Jeju City hotels in a practical order after the final stop. Winter road conditions are checked before the return drive, and the guide may use a lower-altitude route if the mountain road is icy or closed.

sources: AtoC Korea operations notes (Jeju winter return-route protocol)
Jeju Bus Information System (Bus 600 + Bus 100 airport connections)
Jeju Provincial Government (Highway 1135 + Route 16 winter alternates)

routePhases.description: Jeju City → southwest interior to Hallasan viewpoint.
Hallasan viewpoint then southwest tangerine orchard for hands-on picking.
Camellia Hill — the winter headline stop.

routeShapeIntro.title: How this day flows
routeShapeIntro.subtitle: A winter-only southwest loop. Jeju in December–February is mild on the coast (3–10°C) but snowy on Hallasan's upper slopes — a contrast that doesn't exist anywhere else in Korea. The tour pairs the snow viewpoint with two seasonal-peak experiences (red camellia in full bloom, gyul orchards at harvest) plus the standard south-coast UNESCO sites that work year-round. Most international visitors miss winter Jeju entirely; this is the route that makes the case.

bestFor: Travelers visiting Jeju in December-February who want all three winter-only stops (snow, camellia, tangerine) in one day
Photographers chasing camellia bloom, Hallasan snow, and tangerine harvest in a single frame range
Couples and adult families wanting a hands-on cultural moment (orchard picking)
First-time winter-Jeju visitors — the alternative is splitting these stops across two tour days
Travelers who prefer fixed-itinerary structure with cold-weather operational support
lessIdealFor: Travelers visiting outside December-February — camellia and tangerine harvest are season-locked
Visitors who want deep Hallasan trekking (this stop is viewpoint-only)
Travelers needing wheelchair access at every stop (Cheonjeyeon has ≈ 80 stairs)
Same-day cruise passengers — winter operations have weather-dependent stop substitutions that complicate sailaway timing
Travelers without warm clothing — Hallasan can be 5-10°C colder than coastal Jeju with wind

routeLogicSections.title: Why this route is built this way

items.label: Three winter-only stops in one day
items.detail: Snow + camellia + tangerine harvest is a uniquely Jeju winter combination — none of these are available outside December–February. Doing all three independently means renting a car (in winter), booking a tangerine farm in advance, and timing the Hallasan viewpoint right. The tour collapses all that into one coordinated 9-hour day.

items.label: Camellia Hill is at its annual peak
items.detail: Camellia Hill is good year-round, but in winter (its namesake season) it's at its absolute best — 6,000+ trees across 500 varieties from 80 countries in synchronized bloom. Other gardens are dormant. This single stop justifies the day for anyone who values winter flowers.

items.label: Hands-on tangerine picking, not a photo stop
items.detail: Most flower-and-citrus tours include a 'tangerine cafe' visit where you buy a single fruit. This route uses a working orchard where you actually pick, taste-test, and take 1 kg home. The orchard fee (₩5,000) is paid on-site to the farmer.

items.label: No hiking required, but you'll see snow
items.detail: Many visitors want winter scenery without the gear, fitness, or risk of an actual mountain hike. The Hallasan viewpoint stop along the 1100 Road delivers snow views from a heated vehicle and a brief walk — perfect for travelers who want the photograph without the climb.

routeLogicSections.title: How the walking load actually feels

items.label: Hallasan viewpoint is mostly drive-up
items.detail: A roadside stop or a short flat walk depending on weather and road conditions. This is not a hike.

items.label: Camellia Hill is large but flat
items.detail: About 4-6 km of paved garden paths total; most travelers walk 1-2 km of selected routes. Senior-friendly with paved surfaces throughout.

items.label: Cheonjeyeon stairs are the only demanding section
items.detail: The upper viewing platform requires no stairs and can substitute if the second-tier descent is too much. About 80 stairs to the lower tiers and back.

items.label: Hallasan 1100 Road closes in heavy snow
items.detail: Typically 1-3 days per winter. The guide switches to Sanbangsan Mountain or the O'sulloc Tea Museum, both nearby.

items.label: Camellia Hill peaks in January
items.detail: December starts the bloom; February still strong through mid-month. January is the absolute peak for synchronized flowering across the 250 varieties.

items.label: Tangerine harvest tapers in February
items.detail: December and January are the strongest picking months. By mid-February some orchards close the picking experience as fruit volume drops.

practicalAccordionItems.title: What's included
practicalAccordionItems.preview: Heated vehicle, licensed English/Chinese-speaking guide-driver, fuel, tolls, parking.
content: Heated vehicle, licensed English/Chinese-speaking guide-driver, fuel, tolls, parking.
Not included: attraction admissions (Camellia Hill ₩10,000, Cheonjeyeon Falls ₩2,500, Jusangjeolli ₩2,000), tangerine-picking fee ₩5,000 (paid on-site to the farmer), lunch, tips.
Total typical out-of-pocket per person: ₩25,000–₩35,000.

practicalAccordionItems.title: Pickup & drop-off
practicalAccordionItems.preview: Free hotel pickup within Jeju City.
content: Free hotel pickup within Jeju City.
Outside Jeju City (Seogwipo, Aewol, Seongsan) pickup adds ₩70,000 paid to the driver.
Confirm pickup location at booking.
Jeju Airport pickup also free if arranged.

practicalAccordionItems.title: Walking & terrain
content: Light to moderate.
The Hallasan viewpoint is a roadside stop or short flat walk.
Camellia Hill is large but flat with paved paths.
Cheonjeyeon Falls has stairs to the lower viewpoint.
Jusangjeolli has a boardwalk with railings.
Comfortable closed-toe shoes recommended.

practicalAccordionItems.title: What to bring (winter Jeju)
practicalAccordionItems.preview: Warm coat, gloves, scarf — Hallasan can be 5–10°C colder than coastal Jeju, with wind.
content: Warm coat, gloves, scarf — Hallasan can be 5–10°C colder than coastal Jeju, with wind.
Closed-toe shoes (no sandals — orchard floor is muddy).
Camera.
Cash for tangerine-picking fee (₩5,000) and admissions.
Pre-purchased gloves available at major sites if you forget.

practicalAccordionItems.title: Cancellation
practicalAccordionItems.preview: Free cancellation up to 24 hours before pickup.
content: Free cancellation up to 24 hours before pickup.
Same-day cancellations and no-shows are non-refundable.
Heavy snow on Hallasan may close the 1100 Road — in that case the route adjusts to skip the snow viewpoint and add an alternate stop.

practicalWeatherStatic._seasonalSummary: Jeju winter (Dec–Feb) is the mildest in Korea — coastal areas hover at 3–10°C — but Hallasan's upper slopes are 5–10°C colder with snow possible. Wind is the dominant factor at the Hallasan viewpoint.

_seasonalTemperatures.notes: Mild for Korea; coat needed but rarely freezing.
Wind chill significant; snow likely Jan–Feb.
Sheltered low-altitude valley; pick-your-own works fine in winter.

seasonalVariations.description: Camellia bloom begins, tangerine harvest peak. Less snow on Hallasan typically.
Camellia at absolute peak. Most likely month for snow on Hallasan. Tangerine still in season.
Camellia still strong through mid-month. Snow likely. Tangerine season ending.
Tour does not run in summer — see East Hydrangea Festival tour or autumn Pink Muhly tour for seasonal alternates.

staticQuestions.question: Will I actually see snow?
staticQuestions.answer: Snow on Hallasan is common in winter, especially in January, but it cannot be promised on every visit. The viewpoint route still offers strong winter mountain scenery even on snowless days. Camellia and tangerine stops are weather-independent.

staticQuestions.question: Are admissions and the picking fee included?
staticQuestions.answer: No. Budget about ₩25,000–₩35,000 per person for admissions plus ₩5,000 for the tangerine-picking fee paid on-site at the orchard.

staticQuestions.question: Can children pick tangerines?
staticQuestions.answer: Yes — tangerine picking is family-friendly and children especially enjoy it. Clippers and baskets are provided. Children pay the same orchard fee as adults; under-3s typically free.

staticQuestions.question: Is this tour available in summer?
staticQuestions.answer: No — the camellia and tangerine stops are winter-specific. For summer, see the East Hydrangea Festival tour. For autumn, see the Pink Muhly + Tangerine tour.

staticQuestions.question: What if Hallasan is closed for snow?
staticQuestions.answer: Heavy snow can close the 1100 Road for safety. In that case, the guide swaps in an alternate stop — typically Sanbangsan Mountain or the Osulloc Tea Museum, both nearby. Camellia Hill and the orchard remain on the itinerary.

staticQuestions.question: How is this different from the East Winter tour?
staticQuestions.answer: Our Eastern Winter tour covers Seongsan Ilchulbong (UNESCO Sunrise Peak), the Haenyeo women-diver show, and the lava-tube cave. This Southwest version covers Hallasan snow + Camellia Hill + south-coast UNESCO sites (Jusangjeolli, Cheonjeyeon). Choose East for UNESCO peak and culture; Southwest for camellia headline and winter mountain views.

staticQuestions.question: Is this a good choice for first-time visitors to Jeju in winter?
staticQuestions.answer: Yes — it covers the most distinctive stops in the area in a single balanced day.

staticQuestions.question: Is the walking difficult?
staticQuestions.answer: Light to moderate. The Hallasan viewpoint is roadside or a short flat walk (depending on weather). Camellia Hill has 4-6 km of optional paved garden paths — most travelers walk 1-2 km. The tangerine orchard is flat ground. Cheonjeyeon has 80+ stairs. Closed-toe shoes essential — orchard floor is muddy in winter.

staticQuestions.question: Is this tour suitable for families?
staticQuestions.answer: Yes — tangerine picking is a family highlight. Camellia Hill has paved paths suitable for most ages. The Hallasan viewpoint is a quick stop. Kids should be dressed for temperatures 5-10°C colder than coastal Jeju on the mountain.

staticQuestions.question: Can older adults join comfortably?
staticQuestions.answer: Yes for active seniors who can handle cold weather and stairs at Cheonjeyeon. The Hallasan viewpoint is roadside (no climb). Camellia Hill paths are paved. Less ideal for travelers needing snow-grip mobility aids in icy conditions.

staticQuestions.question: What's the recommended minimum age?
staticQuestions.answer: Age 6+ recommended. Kids enjoy hands-on tangerine picking; clippers and baskets provided. Camellia Hill is large but kids can walk shorter loops. Cheonjeyeon stairs are skippable. Younger children join with carriers and warm gear.

itineraryStops.name: Hallasan viewpoint (Eorimok or 1100 Wetland)
itineraryStops.description: Drive up the southwest face of Hallasan (Korea's highest peak at 1,947 m) to a viewpoint along the 1100 Road or the Eorimok trailhead. In winter, the upper slopes are dusted with snow even when coastal Jeju is in single-digit Celsius. This is a viewpoint stop, not a hike — bring layers, but no special gear needed.
highlights: Hallasan snow viewpoint
Korea's highest peak (1,947 m)
1100 Road or Eorimok
No hiking required
timeUsed: Main attraction exploration with photo time
itineraryStops.whyOnRoute: Drive-up access to Hallasan's UNESCO highlands without committing to a 4-hour summit hike. This is the only realistic Hallasan-snow option for a winter day-tour.

visitBasics.closed: 1100 Highway may close temporarily during heavy snow (Dec–Feb)
visitBasics.walking: Easy — 1 km wooden boardwalk; flat, accessible

smartNotes.photo: Boardwalk leading into the wetland with Hallasan in the distance is the iconic shot; autumn (October) gives golden grass colors
smartNotes.facilities: Visitor center with snacks and information
smartNotes.tip: Bring layers — temperature is 5–10°C cooler than coastal Jeju; check road conditions in winter; combine with Cheonjeyeon Falls if descending south

itineraryStops.name: Tangerine (gyul) picking experience
itineraryStops.description: Hands-on tangerine harvest at a working Jeju orchard. December through February is peak season — Jeju's mandarins are the island's most famous export, and almost every household had a tangerine tree historically. You're given clippers and a basket; eat as many as you want on-site, take 1 kg home for ₩5,000 (paid on-site).
highlights: Pick-your-own gyul
Eat on-site, no limit
Take 1 kg home (₩5,000 fee)
Working family orchard
timeUsed: Pick fresh Jeju tangerines (gyul) at a local orchard
Take home a small bag (typically included or low-cost add-on)
itineraryStops.whyOnRoute: Winter-only signature experience that makes this seasonal route special. Jeju gyul (tangerine) is the island's flagship agricultural product and harvest season runs Nov-Feb, making this the strongest hands-on cultural moment on cold-month tours.

_poi_meta.note: No POI factsheet match; v6 visitBasics/convenience/smartNotes retained as-is. Recommend manual verification before publication.

highlights: Heuk-dwaeji BBQ
Local southwest restaurant
Vegetarian options

_poi_meta.note: Operational stop (lunch); identified by name pattern. visitBasics/convenience/smartNotes were not factsheet-derived.

itineraryStops.name: Camellia Hill — Winter Peak
itineraryStops.description: The headline winter stop. Camellia Hill has 6,000+ camellia trees representing 500 varieties from 80 countries — most loved in winter, when other gardens are dormant. Peak bloom is December through February. The site also has a hydrangea tunnel (off-season in winter), greenhouse exhibits, and a Snoopy Garden adjacent.
highlights: 6,000+ camellia trees
Peak bloom Dec–Feb
250 camellia varieties
Greenhouse + Snoopy Garden
timeUsed: Main attraction exploration with photo time
itineraryStops.whyOnRoute: Camellia Hill at its annual peak (December-February) is the headline reason this route exists. The synchronous winter bloom of 6,000+ camellia trees (500 varieties from 80 countries) is a uniquely winter-Jeju moment.

visitBasics.admission: Adult 10,000 KRW / Teen 8,000 KRW / Child 7,000 KRW (approximate)
visitBasics.walking: Easy to moderate — gentle hill paths; mostly stroller-accessible

convenience.restroom: Available at entrance and within garden

smartNotes.photo: Hydrangea tunnel in June; pink muhly grass field in October; camellia varieties peak Nov–Apr
smartNotes.facilities: Indoor greenhouse café, gift shop, picnic areas
smartNotes.tip: Visit before 10am or after 4pm in peak season to avoid crowds; pair with Osulloc (10 min drive)

itineraryStops.name: Jusangjeolli Cliff (Daepo Columnar Joints)
itineraryStops.description: Hexagonal columnar lava cliffs on the south coast — formed when Hallasan's lava cooled rapidly on contact with the sea. The geometric cliff face is one of Jeju's most photographed coastal features. Boardwalk and railings; flat walking.
highlights: Hexagonal lava columns
South-coast cliff walk
Photo viewpoint
timeUsed: Main attraction exploration with photo time
itineraryStops.whyOnRoute: Korea's most-photographed columnar lava feature, and the route's strongest UNESCO Geopark stop. Winter brings dramatic surf at the cliff base.

visitBasics.walking: Easy — flat wooden boardwalk along cliff; total walk 15–20 min

smartNotes.photo: Best with high tide and rough seas — waves smashing into the columns make dramatic shots; golden hour gives best column-color contrast
smartNotes.facilities: Small geological exhibition near entrance; souvenir shop
smartNotes.tip: Get there before 11am or after 4pm to avoid tour-bus crowds; combine with Jungmun Saekdal Beach (5 min) for a half-day

itineraryStops.name: Cheonjeyeon Falls + Seonimgyo Bridge
itineraryStops.description: A three-tier waterfall in a forested valley, locally called the 'Pond of the Gods' — legend has seven nymphs descending from heaven to bathe here. The Seonimgyo Bridge above the gorge is decorated with seven nymph carvings. Easy walk along boardwalk paths.
highlights: Three-tier waterfall
Pond of the Gods legend
Seonimgyo Bridge of Seven Nymphs
timeUsed: Main attraction exploration with photo time
itineraryStops.whyOnRoute: Three-tier waterfall and the Seonimgyo Bridge of Seven Nymphs — Jeju's most-photographed forest waterfall stop. Winter flow is lower but bridge photography is at its clearest.

visitBasics.walking: Moderate — many stairs through the valley; not stroller-friendly; mobility-difficult

convenience.restroom: At entrance/parking area only — none past the ticket office

smartNotes.photo: The second tier from its viewing deck is the iconic 30m waterfall shot; Seonimgyo Bridge with seven nymphs is also striking
smartNotes.facilities: Small souvenir shop and café at entrance
smartNotes.tip: The first tier is dry except after heavy rain — don't be surprised if it's not flowing; allow 1.5–2 hours to see all three tiers; do NOT confuse with Cheonjiyeon Falls (different falls in Seogwipo)

routePhases.description: Jeju City → southwest interior to Hallasan viewpoint.
Hallasan viewpoint then southwest tangerine orchard for hands-on picking.
Camellia Hill — the winter headline stop.

routeShapeIntro.title: How this day flows
routeShapeIntro.subtitle: A winter-only southwest loop. Jeju in December–February is mild on the coast (3–10°C) but snowy on Hallasan's upper slopes — a contrast that doesn't exist anywhere else in Korea. The tour pairs the snow viewpoint with two seasonal-peak experiences (red camellia in full bloom, gyul orchards at harvest) plus the standard south-coast UNESCO sites that work year-round. Most international visitors miss winter Jeju entirely; this is the route that makes the case.

bestFor: Travelers visiting Jeju in December-February who want all three winter-only stops (snow, camellia, tangerine) in one day
Photographers chasing camellia bloom, Hallasan snow, and tangerine harvest in a single frame range
Couples and adult families wanting a hands-on cultural moment (orchard picking)
First-time winter-Jeju visitors — the alternative is splitting these stops across two tour days
Travelers who prefer fixed-itinerary structure with cold-weather operational support
lessIdealFor: Travelers visiting outside December-February — camellia and tangerine harvest are season-locked
Visitors who want deep Hallasan trekking (this stop is viewpoint-only)
Travelers needing wheelchair access at every stop (Cheonjeyeon has ≈ 80 stairs)
Same-day cruise passengers — winter operations have weather-dependent stop substitutions that complicate sailaway timing
Travelers without warm clothing — Hallasan can be 5-10°C colder than coastal Jeju with wind

routeLogicSections.title: Why this route is built this way

items.label: Three winter-only stops in one day
items.detail: Snow + camellia + tangerine harvest is a uniquely Jeju winter combination — none of these are available outside December–February. Doing all three independently means renting a car (in winter), booking a tangerine farm in advance, and timing the Hallasan viewpoint right. The tour collapses all that into one coordinated 9-hour day.

items.label: Camellia Hill is at its annual peak
items.detail: Camellia Hill is good year-round, but in winter (its namesake season) it's at its absolute best — 6,000+ trees across 500 varieties from 80 countries in synchronized bloom. Other gardens are dormant. This single stop justifies the day for anyone who values winter flowers.

items.label: Hands-on tangerine picking, not a photo stop
items.detail: Most flower-and-citrus tours include a 'tangerine cafe' visit where you buy a single fruit. This route uses a working orchard where you actually pick, taste-test, and take 1 kg home. The orchard fee (₩5,000) is paid on-site to the farmer.

items.label: No hiking required, but you'll see snow
items.detail: Many visitors want winter scenery without the gear, fitness, or risk of an actual mountain hike. The Hallasan viewpoint stop along the 1100 Road delivers snow views from a heated vehicle and a brief walk — perfect for travelers who want the photograph without the climb.

routeLogicSections.title: How the walking load actually feels

items.label: Hallasan viewpoint is mostly drive-up
items.detail: A roadside stop or a short flat walk depending on weather and road conditions. This is not a hike.

items.label: Camellia Hill is large but flat
items.detail: About 4-6 km of paved garden paths total; most travelers walk 1-2 km of selected routes. Senior-friendly with paved surfaces throughout.

items.label: Cheonjeyeon stairs are the only demanding section
items.detail: The upper viewing platform requires no stairs and can substitute if the second-tier descent is too much. About 80 stairs to the lower tiers and back.

items.label: Hallasan 1100 Road closes in heavy snow
items.detail: Typically 1-3 days per winter. The guide switches to Sanbangsan Mountain or the O'sulloc Tea Museum, both nearby.

items.label: Camellia Hill peaks in January
items.detail: December starts the bloom; February still strong through mid-month. January is the absolute peak for synchronized flowering across the 250 varieties.

items.label: Tangerine harvest tapers in February
items.detail: December and January are the strongest picking months. By mid-February some orchards close the picking experience as fruit volume drops.

practicalAccordionItems.title: What's included
practicalAccordionItems.content: Heated vehicle, licensed English/Chinese-speaking guide-driver, fuel, tolls, parking. Not included: attraction admissions (Camellia Hill ₩10,000, Cheonjeyeon Falls ₩2,500, Jusangjeolli ₩2,000), tangerine-picking fee ₩5,000 (paid on-site to the farmer), lunch, tips. Total typical out-of-pocket per person: ₩25,000–₩35,000.

practicalAccordionItems.title: Pickup & drop-off
practicalAccordionItems.content: Free hotel pickup within Jeju City. Outside Jeju City (Seogwipo, Aewol, Seongsan) pickup adds ₩70,000 paid to the driver. Confirm pickup location at booking. Jeju Airport pickup also free if arranged.

practicalAccordionItems.title: Walking & terrain
practicalAccordionItems.content: Light to moderate. The Hallasan viewpoint is a roadside stop or short flat walk. Camellia Hill is large but flat with paved paths. Cheonjeyeon Falls has stairs to the lower viewpoint. Jusangjeolli has a boardwalk with railings. Comfortable closed-toe shoes recommended.

practicalAccordionItems.title: What to bring (winter Jeju)
practicalAccordionItems.content: Warm coat, gloves, scarf — Hallasan can be 5–10°C colder than coastal Jeju, with wind. Closed-toe shoes (no sandals — orchard floor is muddy). Camera. Cash for tangerine-picking fee (₩5,000) and admissions. Pre-purchased gloves available at major sites if you forget.

practicalAccordionItems.title: Cancellation
practicalAccordionItems.content: Free cancellation up to 24 hours before pickup. Same-day cancellations and no-shows are non-refundable. Heavy snow on Hallasan may close the 1100 Road — in that case the route adjusts to skip the snow viewpoint and add an alternate stop.

staticQuestions.question: Is this tour available in summer?
staticQuestions.answer: No — the camellia and tangerine stops are winter-specific. For summer, see the East Hydrangea Festival tour. For autumn, see the Pink Muhly + Tangerine tour.

staticQuestions.question: What if Hallasan is closed for snow?
staticQuestions.answer: Heavy snow can close the 1100 Road for safety. In that case, the guide swaps in an alternate stop — typically Sanbangsan Mountain or the Osulloc Tea Museum, both nearby. Camellia Hill and the orchard remain on the itinerary.

staticQuestions.question: How is this different from the East Winter tour?
staticQuestions.answer: Our Eastern Winter tour covers Seongsan Ilchulbong (UNESCO Sunrise Peak), the Haenyeo women-diver show, and the lava-tube cave. This Southwest version covers Hallasan snow + Camellia Hill + south-coast UNESCO sites (Jusangjeolli, Cheonjeyeon). Choose East for UNESCO peak and culture; Southwest for camellia headline and winter mountain views.

staticQuestions.question: Is this a good choice for first-time visitors to Jeju in winter?
staticQuestions.answer: Yes — it covers the most distinctive stops in the area in a single balanced day.

staticQuestions.question: Is the walking difficult?
staticQuestions.answer: Light to moderate. The Hallasan viewpoint is roadside or a short flat walk (depending on weather). Camellia Hill has 4-6 km of optional paved garden paths — most travelers walk 1-2 km. The tangerine orchard is flat ground. Cheonjeyeon has 80+ stairs. Closed-toe shoes essential — orchard floor is muddy in winter.

staticQuestions.question: Is this tour suitable for families?
staticQuestions.answer: Yes — tangerine picking is a family highlight. Camellia Hill has paved paths suitable for most ages. The Hallasan viewpoint is a quick stop. Kids should be dressed for temperatures 5-10°C colder than coastal Jeju on the mountain.

staticQuestions.question: Can older adults join comfortably?
staticQuestions.answer: Yes for active seniors who can handle cold weather and stairs at Cheonjeyeon. The Hallasan viewpoint is roadside (no climb). Camellia Hill paths are paved. Less ideal for travelers needing snow-grip mobility aids in icy conditions.

staticQuestions.question: What's the recommended minimum age?
staticQuestions.answer: Age 6+ recommended. Kids enjoy hands-on tangerine picking; clippers and baskets provided. Camellia Hill is large but kids can walk shorter loops. Cheonjeyeon stairs are skippable. Younger children join with carriers and warm gear.

v17_schema_unification.description: Phase 1: Standardized matching_profile scores to the current 0-1 float scale used by match_tours.matching_profile.

v17_batch5_enhancement.description: Batch 5 deep enhancement — descriptions raised to 700-1300+ chars; KB-backed metadata on all attraction stops; matching_profile recalibrated with verified-fact 0-1 float scores. Time-weighted indoor_ratio per Fix 1 methodology.

_score_audit._methodology: 0-1 float scale. Indoor ratio computed time-weighted. This is a winter-focused outdoor tour with intentionally high outdoor exposure for snow/cold experience.

unesco_fit.reasoning: Verified: Hallasan is UNESCO World Natural Heritage (2007). 1/6 stops UNESCO. 0.55 calibrated.

walking_level.reasoning: Verified: Hallasan Eorimok requires 1-2 km mountain trail walking (with crampons winter); other stops moderate. 0.65 — the Hallasan component pushes walking level up.

scenic_level.reasoning: Verified: snow-covered Hallasan plateau + tangerine orchards + camellia hill + columnar cliff + three-tier waterfall = catalog-leading winter scenic diversity. 0.95.

photo_level.reasoning: Verified: snow + red camellia + orange tangerines + black basalt + waterfall = catalog-leading visual contrast palette. 0.95 — uniquely photogenic combination available only Dec-Feb.

iconic_landmark_fit.reasoning: Verified: Hallasan UNESCO + Camellia Hill regional icon + Jusangjeolli Natural Monument + Cheonjeyeon iconic 3-tier falls. 4 strong icons. 0.85.

indoor_ratio.reasoning: MAJOR CORRECTION — winter tour is intentionally outdoor-heavy: 67.5 indoor / 390 total = 0.17 with lunch; 19.5/330 = 0.06 without. Catalog 0.10 calibrated. The Hallasan + tangerine orchards + Jusangjeolli are all 0-10% indoor.

weather_sensitivity.reasoning: VERIFIED HIGHEST — winter snow tour completely weather-dependent: blizzard cancels Hallasan, ice closes orchards, rain reduces camellia photos. Highest weather sensitivity in catalog along with cherry blossom tours.

rain_fit.reasoning: DOWNGRADED — winter tour particularly rain-vulnerable: Hallasan trail in rain becomes dangerous, tangerine picking moves to greenhouse only, Jusangjeolli loses dramatic photo. 0.30 honest assessment.

snow_fit.reasoning: UPGRADED — this is THE catalog tour designed for snow. Snow on Hallasan is the FEATURE (not bug). Camellia red + snow white is signature. 0.95 — highest snow-fit in catalog.

first_time_fit.reasoning: Verified: excellent winter first-Jeju but season-locked to mid-Dec to mid-Jan for Hallabong window + camellia winter peak + best snow. 0.75 calibrated for narrow season.

value_for_money_fit.reasoning: Verified fees: Hallasan FREE + Tangerine ~₩7,000-15,000 + Camellia ₩9,000 + Jusangjeolli ₩2,000 + Cheonjeyeon ₩2,500 = ~₩22,500/adult. Hallasan free is a major value driver. 0.75.

young_kids_fit.reasoning: Verified: tangerine picking is highly kid-friendly + Camellia + Jusangjeolli. Hallasan trail + winter cold limit youngest kids. 0.65.

senior_fit.reasoning: UPGRADED — 1100 Wetland alternative option makes it senior-accessible; tangerine + Camellia + Jusangjeolli boardwalk all flat. Eorimok hike is the one challenge for seniors. 0.50.

stroller_fit.reasoning: Hallasan trail not stroller-friendly (snow + uneven terrain); other stops stroller-OK. 0.45 calibrated for the Hallasan limitation.

mobility_friendly_fit.reasoning: Same as stroller — Hallasan winter trail is the mobility limitation; 1100 Wetland boardwalk alternative helps. 0.45.

active_fit.reasoning: UPGRADED — winter mountain hike is the active component. Catalog-leading active winter content. 0.85.

nature_fit.reasoning: UPGRADED — UNESCO mountain + agricultural orchard + flower garden + columnar cliff + waterfall = catalog-leading winter nature density. 0.95.

experiential_fit.reasoning: Verified: tangerine picking is hands-on agricultural experience — eat-as-you-pick + take-home + 1,500-year tradition narrative. Strongest experiential anchor on the winter route. 0.90.

geological_fit.reasoning: Verified: Hallasan UNESCO volcanic + Jusangjeolli columnar joints. 2 strong geological landmarks. 0.75.

_extended_dimensions_documented_only.note: These dimensions (snow_fit, active_fit, geological_fit, experiential_fit) were assessed during the v17 verified-fact audit but are NOT yet part of the standard 68-key matching_profile. They are preserved here as documentation for future schema extension.
